preFix.title: Wet-felted cat toy balls
preFix.subtitle: A set of four firm merino felt balls, 3 to 4 cm diameter, without catnip or additives
preFix.excerpt: Roll merino roving into compact balls and wet-felt them into dense spheres that bounce and roll without splitting. Each ball uses around 5 g of roving. Makes four balls in under an hour. No filling, no catnip, no synthetic components.
preFix.sourceNotes: Wet-felted wool balls as cat toys are a standard introductory felting project. The rolling-in-the-palm felting method is the classic technique for producing spheres. Observation-led; the method is consistent across guild teaching materials.

content.text: balls are dense enough to bounce and light enough to roll erratically, which suits cats. Made from natural

only: no synthetic fill, no catnip (which can cause digestive upset in cats that chew and ingest it), no buttons or small parts that could be swallowed. The balls are firm enough that they do not disintegrate with normal play but they are not indestructible; supervise use and replace if a cat begins breaking through the surface.

items.name: Merino roving, in two or three colours, 20 g total (5 g per ball)
Hot water and olive-oil soap or wool wash
Bowl of cold water for final rinse

content.text: Pull approximately 5 g of roving from the length and fold it into a rough ball shape, tucking the ends in. Squeeze it firmly between your palms for 30 seconds to compress the fibres into a rounded starting form. The pre-form should be about 4 to 5 cm across; it will shrink during felting.

Dip the pre-form in hot soapy water. Squeeze gently to wet it through, then begin rolling it between your palms using light circular pressure. Do not press hard at first: heavy pressure on an unwet felt will distort rather than sphere. Roll lightly for 2 minutes as the surface begins to felt, then gradually increase the pressure as the ball firms. Add a drop of hot water if the ball cools.
Step 3: Full to a firm sphere

As the ball begins to hold its shape, increase palm pressure and roll faster in tight circular motions. The ball shrinks and firms. After 5 to 8 minutes of active rolling the ball should be 3 to 4 cm in diameter and dense enough to bounce once on a hard floor. Any surface bumps can be smoothed by rolling slowly and pressing from different angles.

Rinse the ball under cold water until water runs clear, then squeeze firmly to remove excess water. Place the balls on a clean towel and leave to air-dry for several hours. Once dry they are ready. Make the remaining balls in the same way, using different colours for variety.

items.fix: Roll in varying circular patterns, changing the orientation of the ball in your palms frequently. A round sphere requires even pressure from all angles.
items.cause: Rolling was in a fixed pattern rather than in all directions
items.problem: The ball is oval or flat-sided rather than round

items.fix: Felt lumpy balls cannot easily be corrected once dry. For future balls, fold the roving evenly and compress it into a smooth pre-form before wetting.
items.cause: The roving was not folded evenly before rolling, leaving thick clumps inside
items.problem: The surface is uneven with lumps that will not smooth out
